title: Somatic Therapy for Imposter Syndrome: When Success Feels Unsafe
excerpt: Discover how somatic therapy addresses imposter syndrome by calming your nervous system and reclaiming safety in success.
body:
Understanding Imposter Syndrome Beyond the Mind

Imposter syndrome is often described as a persistent feeling of intellectual fraudulence, where despite evidence of competence, individuals feel they don’t deserve their success. This phenomenon affects high achievers across professions and can cause chronic anxiety, self-doubt, and emotional exhaustion. But what if the root of imposter syndrome isn't just in your thoughts, but deeply embedded in your body’s nervous system?

Researchers like Bessel van der Kolk have shown that trauma and stress are stored in the body, influencing how we perceive ourselves and the world (van der Kolk, 2014). Similarly, Peter Levine and Stephen Porges emphasize that unresolved nervous system dysregulation can drive feelings of threat and insecurity, even when external situations are objectively safe (Levine, 2010; Porges, 2011). Somatic therapy, which focuses on body-based healing, offers a path to untangle imposter syndrome not through more cognitive reframing alone, but by addressing the body’s felt experience of unsafety.

Why Success Can Feel Unsafe: The Nervous System’s Role

Success triggers a range of complex physiological responses. For many with imposter syndrome, the nervous system interprets achievement as a threat, activating survival responses:

  • Fight/Flight: Anxiety, hypervigilance, or even anger can erupt as the body prepares to defend itself against an imagined danger.
  • Freeze or Shutdown: Feelings of numbness, disconnection, or paralysis may follow, as the body conserves energy to survive what it perceives as an overwhelming threat.

Deb Dana explains these as autonomic nervous system patterns that impact our social engagement and sense of safety (Dana, 2018). When you succeed, your nervous system might not register it as a positive signal but as an unsafe state requiring defense. This creates a vicious cycle: the more you achieve, the more your body reacts with stress, reinforcing the belief you’re an imposter.

Somatic Therapy: Reconnecting Body and Mind to Overcome Imposter Syndrome

Somatic therapy involves tuning into bodily sensations, movements, and nervous system cues to process trauma and stress. It’s not about ignoring thoughts but integrating your body’s wisdom with your cognitive understanding. Pat Ogden, a pioneer in somatic psychology, highlights that trauma and negative beliefs are held in muscle tension, posture, and physiological states (Ogden, 2015). By working somatically, you can recalibrate your nervous system’s responses to success.

Key Somatic Tools for Imposter Syndrome

  • Body Awareness: Notice where and how imposter feelings show up physically—chest tightness, stomach knots, shallow breathing.
  • Tracking Sensations: Observe changes in your body as you recall achievements. Is there a contraction, a sinking feeling?
  • Resourcing: Develop internal resources by recalling moments of safety or strength and feeling those sensations in the body.
  • Movement and Breath: Use gentle movements or breathing exercises to discharge trapped energy and shift nervous system states (Levine, 2010).
  • Grounding: Connect with the physical environment through your feet, posture, or touch to anchor your nervous system in the present.

Polyvagal Theory: The Science Behind Feeling Unsafe in Success

Stephen Porges’ Polyvagal Theory offers a framework for understanding the nervous system’s role in imposter syndrome. It describes three neural circuits:

  • Ventral Vagal: The social engagement system associated with safety, connection, and calm.
  • Sympathetic: The fight-or-flight activation preparing for action.
  • Dorsal Vagal: The shutdown or freeze system linked to immobilization and dissociation.

When success feels unsafe, the nervous system may default to sympathetic arousal (anxiety) or dorsal vagal shutdown (numbness), blocking access to the ventral vagal pathway, which allows for feeling safe and connected to self and others (Porges, 2011). Somatic therapy aims to strengthen ventral vagal tone, enabling you to tolerate success without triggering survival responses.

Somatic Exercises to Begin Your Healing Journey

Here are practical, research-backed somatic exercises to start regulating your nervous system around success:

  • Body Scan: Sit quietly and scan from head to toe, noticing tension or discomfort without judgment. This builds body awareness (Ogden, 2015).
  • Safe Place Visualization: Imagine a place where you feel completely safe and relaxed. Notice the bodily sensations associated with this safety (van der Kolk, 2014).
  • Resourcing Through Touch: Place your hand on your heart or belly and breathe slowly, inviting calm through tactile grounding (Dana, 2018).
  • Pendulation: Shift attention between areas of tension and areas of ease in your body to help discharge stress and restore balance (Levine, 2010).
  • Tracking Success Sensations: Recall a recent success and observe how your body reacts. Allow yourself to stay with the sensations, using breath or movement to soften tension.

The Role of Attachment and Internal Parts in Imposter Syndrome

Richard Schwartz’s Internal Family Systems (IFS) model complements somatic therapy by identifying internal parts that hold imposter beliefs, such as the “perfectionist” or “critic” (Schwartz, 1995). These parts often carry protective roles born from early attachment wounds.

Gabor Maté

Why Traditional Therapy Alone May Not Resolve Imposter Syndrome

Many people have tried talk therapy to address imposter syndrome with limited success. Cognitive approaches can change beliefs intellectually but often miss the embodied experience of threat. Francine Shapiro

“When the body perceives success as a threat, no amount of positive thinking will override the nervous system’s survival instincts.” — The Oracle Lover

Integrating Somatic Therapy Into Your Self-Directed Healing

To work with imposter syndrome somatically, start by cultivating curiosity and patience with your body’s responses. Journaling sensations alongside thoughts can deepen insight. Regular somatic exercises support nervous system regulation and build resilience.

Consider combining somatic practices with modalities like mindfulness, IFS, or EMDR if accessible. The goal is to create a felt sense of safety in your body when you succeed, breaking the cycle where achievement triggers fear or paralysis.

Ultimately, somatic therapy helps you reclaim your inherent worthiness from the inside out, making success feel not just possible but safe.

References

  • Dana, D. (2018). The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy: Engaging the Rhythm of Regulation. W.W. Norton & Company.
  • Levine, P. (2010). In an Unspoken Voice: How the Body Releases Trauma and Restores Goodness. North Atlantic Books.
  • Maté, G. (2018). In the Realm of Hungry Ghosts: Close Encounters with Addiction. North Atlantic Books.
  • Ogden, P. (2015). Sensorimotor Psychotherapy: Interventions for Trauma and Attachment. W.W. Norton & Company.
  • Porges, S. (2011). The Polyvagal Theory: Neurophysiological Foundations of Emotions, Attachment, Communication, and Self-Regulation. W.W. Norton & Company.
  • Schwartz, R. (1995). Internal Family Systems Therapy. Guilford Press.
  • Shapiro, F. (2018). Eye Movement Desensitization and Reprocessing (EMDR) Therapy, Third Edition: Basic Principles, Protocols, and Procedures. Guilford Press.
  • van der Kolk, B. (2014). The Body Keeps the Score: Brain, Mind, and Body in the Healing of Trauma. Viking.

faqs.q: How does somatic therapy differ from traditional talk therapy for imposter syndrome?
faqs.a: Somatic therapy focuses on the body's sensations and nervous system responses, addressing implicit trauma stored in the body, while talk therapy primarily targets cognitive patterns.

faqs.q: Can somatic therapy help if I haven’t experienced overt trauma?
faqs.a: Yes. Even without explicit trauma, chronic stress or early attachment disruptions can dysregulate the nervous system, making somatic therapy beneficial for feeling safer in success.

faqs.q: How long does it take to see results with somatic therapy for imposter syndrome?
faqs.a: Results vary, but many people notice shifts in bodily awareness and nervous system regulation within weeks of consistent practice, with deeper changes unfolding over months.

faqs.q: Are somatic therapy exercises safe to do on my own?
faqs.a: Most gentle somatic exercises are safe to practice independently, but if you experience overwhelming emotions or sensations, it’s important to seek guidance from a trained somatic therapist.

metaDescription: Calm your nervous system and overcome imposter syndrome with somatic therapy. Learn body-based tools for feeling safe in your success.
author: The Oracle Lover
